commentBody: That SE corner was fiendish for me
Other than the SE corner (never heard of CRAB LOUIE), that was a fairly easy Thursday. I didn’t even look at the theme yet.

What does a math teacher sing on New Year's Eve? Auld lang sine. Why can't he get a home loan? Nobody will cosine. Why can't his students follow his lectures? He's always off on a tangent. He'd like to stop punning, but it's a compulsion, so of cosecant.

Hmmm. Trying again. Maybe changing words?

Not a tricky Thursday, but a funny one.
Nothing fishy, of course.

(Was 38A knocking yesterday's puzzle?)

Our columnist asks for other clues like [Toys for tots, perhaps] for TYPO.  OK...

[Care for cars?]
[Cars for cats, say]
[Ore, for one?]
[Tears for Fears, e.g.]
[Four for for, for one]
[Nooks for books, maybe]
[Run for fun, perhaps]

as well as

[Embarassing mistake]
[Bad character]
[Unlucky strike?]
[Result of a bad stroke]

Note:  These have only appeared since the start of Will Shortz's tenure.  Prior to that, clues for TYPO were dry ones like [Printing error] or [Steno's slip].
I generally find that a puzzle that has both shaded squares and circles has one gimmick too many, unless the payoff is phenomenal.

I found that really hard!  And proportionately satisfying!

Now I gotta go back and make sure that the perpendicular letters in circles didn't fight with the shaded fish they ihntersected.

I love a mysterious gimmick, and this definitely qualifies.

I didn’t have much on my initial pass through the acrosses and I thought something fishy was going on. And I was right!  And I was hooked.  Fortunately the downs were much easier and I was able to reel this one in without too much agita.  Not the trickiest Thursday ever, but cute enough.

Our columnist writes:
"I hope there is a door — for either film fans or fish fans — to help crack it."

The third door is to solve the Across and Down answers -- knowing them or filling with crosses -- and then discover the fish and the films.

Can you believe I've gone through my whole life not realizing the tree creatures in Lord of the Rings were tonsillectomy specialists?

What a great TIL!

What am I going to find out next?  Tom Bombadil was an OB-GYN?  That orcs were proctologists?

This theme, for me, was more of "appreciate after completion" than "help solving puzzle". This is not a criticism-- I love both -- but by the time I figured the theme out, the only themed across left was POP____T, which could have been any number of four letter fish. 

In the top left I had CRude before CRASS, but then _uBB_ didn't make sense. Put in RABBI and let CRA__ percolate and it came out fine. 

I still hate not being able to refer to the grid while commenting (unless I navigate separately to Wordplay in my browser, which is a pain even with bookmark). Grumble.

So this was definitely a constructor’s puzzle, and for me the hook was trying to guess what on earth Oscar winners had to do with fish, and marveling at a clever grid and execution. So kudos for that, even if I’m a little disappointed because CHAR, for me at least, was a pretty obscure fish. Still this had a lot of complexity between the hooks and making sure the fish were embedded seamlessly into the theme so who am I to complain? Maybe the last fish should have been “boWANDArrow” since “A Fish Called Wanda” really was Oscar bait for Kevin Kline.
But, realistically, you have to admit that otherwise this entirely solved like a themeless (and not a hard one at that) so I have to suggest that it is more suited for a Wednesday. It could have used some more misdirecting clues although I liked AUDIT and TYPO (although inevitably someone will point out if it’s deliberate for a clue is it really a TYPO?) 
I would like to point out that KITTLES are not only for rabbis and are worn by many religious Jewish men on many occasions. Yes the clue said “maybe” but it still seems as misleading as saying the same thing about a yarmulke.
On that note for those of you who like to rely on AI, I would like to point out that when I asked AI to verify if “kittles were only for rabbis” (ok I did misspell it) it told me with a straight AI face that “anyone can eat Skittles, although they have recently been certified kosher so Rabbis can eat them.”  Phew. Silly Rabbi, Skittles are for kids!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Kugelman and Ezersky are, for this novice, the premiere exemplars of the gritty, black-and-white Dragnet school of grid setting.

I am, in spite of myself, grudgingly learning to appreciate their blue-collar, stick-to-your-ribs fare.

As opposed to the dreamy/trippy/flowy Twin Peaks school.

Which, of course, is embodied in Weintraub and Goldstein.

Nobu every evening would likely grow boring without the occasional outing to Red Lobster.
Great job. Really clever. Fun gimmick.
Yes! A lot of fun. I had to work at it. It never gave me the feeling that I wasn’t going to get it, though. Still a good Thursday puzzle. Thank you.
I thought the tots were playing with TYcO toys. Took me a long time to find/fix that one. Great puzzle!

Today I learned that crab Louis is also called crab Louie.  Although the Ngram Viewer shows that crab Louis has been much more common than crab Louie for most of the past 100 years, although crab Louie has recently become more popular.  I noticed when googling that both Google and Wikipedia seem to prefer crab Louie, and I wonder if that's influenced the recent popularity of crab Louie as the spelling.

I enjoyed the theme today.  A lot of good cluing with entries like DOT BOMB, RHODE, OSCAR BAIT, etc.

I had a ton of trouble in the SE corner.  First not knowing the names of golfers from the 50s, I had BEN HO but there's a dozen common last names that fit that pattern.  On top of that RIEL and OGDEN in the same area.  Started at that for a while.

I'm also not sure how "Toys for tots" is a TYPO, and that crossed with SLY which was hard to think of what kind of animal "Vulpine" referenced.

But nice gimmick with the combination of the movie with the fish.

I think a minor claim to fame of mine might be that I'm the only person on the planet who's sung Auld Lang SYNE on all Seven Continents. On January 1st of this year, I celebrated NYE on my seventh continent, having drunkenly sung Auld Lang SYNE each time.
I found the initial hints from columnist frustrating — not include the crossword hint numbers. I was able to solve most of the puzzle without help.

Fun theme but obnoxious levels of trivia crossing in multiple areas. 

What's the point? I think I'm going to get into cryptic I'm sick of having to google arbitrary bs.

The amounts of trivia lately has gone full throttle. Not enjoyable.
Missed opportunity to write a clue about Louis RIEL, but a fun theme I totally missed seeing until almost done.

An enjoyable puzzle and super clever construction. I just always feel a little disappointed when a Thursday puzzle doesn’t require me to do anything quirky to solve it. There was no catch in this one (despite all the hooks! lol), just filling in the answers in a straightforward fashion revealed something cool. I like a Thursday that comes with a trick!
The parts of the "hooks" in the shaded squares were hard to see - no wonder they landed those fish! (But I was at sea until I read the explanation.)

I had SALMON for dinner, but those omega-3s don't seem to have done much for my brain, maybe because I had a lot on my mind today. For someone who loves music, I just couldn't wrap my head around _O_ CHART being anything other than a gangster's [hit list.] I went completely blank, not even the crossers could save me, so I came to the column and...TADA! At the start, I was a bit worried when I saw it was a puzzle co-created by Mr. Kugelman because I always find his puzzles super clever but also a workout. If only the salmon had given me the oomph I needed... Still, all's well that ends well. However long it took for me to tackle this puzzle, I enjoyed every minute of it. (And now I get to read the comments while listening to Hall & OATES... https://youtu.be/ccenFp_3kq8?si=cWrOZ7e9T5CFrR-E)

It's silly Thursday. No trick today, but quite silly.
Was 38A mocking yesterday's puzzle?)

Had it finished, but no, something not right still.  The ones I figured were wrong were right after all, silly me, had EMTS instead of ENTS.   HOGAN of course.  It’s so often some obvious dumb mistake at the end.
That’s an impressive construction.

You want typos?

How about--

DAIRYDIARYDAILY     ?

Give up?
DAILYDAIRYDIARY    !!!

That fill... Monday easy almost everywhere, except the SE corner, where the constructors decided to intertwine several trivia entries, including a *golfer* from over 70 years ago. At least for once the theme didn't involve the signature Kugelman dad jokes...

CRABLOUIE crossed with HOGAN and RIELS 🫤🫤

Not puzzling … quizzing. Never heard of the salad and history of golf is particularly esoteric. 

The fishing ‘trick’  was nice. The cluing reasonable for mid to late week.

"Did you see the fishing movie?"
"I'll probably stream it."

("I heard it's reely good!")
Drip? Really?
Kind of a weird little puzzle, but I really liked the clues.

Imagine my delight when I remembered that I have a bank note featuring the temple of Angkor Wat right here in the room with me!

...Now imagine my disappointment when it was no help at all.  XD
TIL that the Cambodian currency gets its name from the Spanish / Portuguese real, just like the Iranian currency. 😊

I recently needed to buy several black polo shirts for work. Since I was just going for whatever was reasonably affordable, I didn't pay much attention to what I bought at the time. About an hour after struggling through the SE corner's trivia, I realized that I was wearing a BEN HOGAN shirt this whole time.

[Fear of a launderer]
Yep. I went for STAIN hook, line and sinker. 
WHAT NERVE! 😂
Enjoyed the puzzle. Took me a good moment to realize the movie title was Patton with George C Scott and not some move I never heard of called Not Tap!!!!
Calling a term that has existed in AAVE for decades and only recently been appropriated “Gen Z slang” is a bizarre choice to put it mildly.

Sean McGowan's piece on different doors to reach the solution gets to the heart of why I love the American crossword format, it's more of a journey than a series of barriers.

It took me a while to fully appreciate it, but getting rid of the unches was Farrar's masterstroke.

This puzzle felt tough as I was going through it, moving around a lot, nibbling away at it. And like others, I found the southeast tricky. But then I finished at 75 percent of my Thursday average. 

Weird.

Or, as someone else might describe it, about 600 percent less than my average time.
I don't think I ever heard the term "Oscar Bait" before so I really wasn't pulled in by the movie and fish clues. Oh well.
Huh. This one must have clicked with me because I crushed it (in terms of my normal Thursday times). I figured I'd come in here and see everyone saying this was an easy one.
“Fear for a launderer” … great!

Since no one else has mentioned this:

The OSCAR BAIT revealer and the “hidden” fish in the grid are thematically similar to a WaPo Sunday puzzle by Evan Birnholz that ran just a few months ago. Granted, the movie titles are handled differently here, “angled” into fishhook shapes as they are.

I’m willing to believe that the NYT accepted this puzzle for publication before the WaPo puzzle ran, but I’m surprised that they published this puzzle so soon after that one. 

I cast no aspersions on the constructors. I’ve had the experience of working on an “original” theme only to see it appear in someone else’s puzzle.

[Moops for Moors, perhaps?] I just happened across that scene again last night. Still very funny. https://youtu.be/f0uYJjDHeDU?si=yidzFAMOjdY5P-NG Challenging without being impossible. My first time through I thought of OSCARBAIT, but I didn't have a hook for it, so I kept going. Never heard of CRABLOUIE, but there were plenty of crosses to get me there. Thanks!

Totally breezed through this puzzle and never even noticed the shaded squares on my printed grid. I did see the "hooks" and got all the movie titles, of course, because they sort of just filled themselves in. I guess I wasn't paying attention to notice that those shaded squares were actually fish. After seeing the totality of the theme that I missed, I'm more impressed, especially because I dislike most puzzles by J.K.

I like the idea of this theme, but it might have had a bit more *zing* with at least one more film/fish cross - perhaps with a film that’s come out more recently (as in after 1990),  Or maybe it would have worked better on a Sunday with more of the theme answers providing visual clues to the overall premise.  
I’ve only seen 2 out of the three films represented as the “hooks” (war movies generally aren’t my thing) and both of those were before I graduated high school - and I’m old enough to need reading glasses.
As a trivia-heavy Thursday with only three theme answers I was left feeling a bit underwhelmed and unsatisfied.  3/5

As soon as I saw the clue "Toys for tots," I immediately knew the answer was TYPO. Why? Because I could have sworn that I'd seen that identical clue and answer in a NYT puzzle not that long ago. But after having read every comment, I guess it hasn't appeared here. Maybe in a WaPo puzzle? That's the only other one I do on a consistent basis on Mondays and sometimes Tuesdays.

Oops! I've made about four comments here using a name I changed to yesterday for commenting on a NYT article. I forgot to change it back. IvanaVindalotto is Times Rita, one and the same! Thank you for your attention to this matter 😊.

A riddle-fest from start to finish; a capital-P puzzle.

Yes, the obvious riddles to crack.  What is the theme?  What are the grayed and circled letters for?  But then there were also tough areas to fill in, tough clues to crack.

Each riddle solved brought pings of pleasure. “Oh, the grayed letters are fish!”  “Oh, the circled letters are hooks!”  “Oh, the first word of [Exchanged words] is an adjective!”  

All this, and yet, maybe, the highlight was [What am I, chopped liver?] for PATE, where my jaw actually OMG-dropped in elation.  

And it was an impressive build.  The hooks alone greatly restricted what answers could surround them.  Not to mention, those hooks had to catch fish, and film titles had to go in as well.  Are you kidding me?  

And even with all that, there was answer loveliness – COME ON MAN, CRAB LOUIE, OSCAR BAIT, WHAT NERVE, RAINMAKER.  There was pop as well, with eight NYT debut answers, all worthy.

I came to the box, Lance and John, curious and hopeful for a stellar Thursday, something clever and something I had to earn, and you two came through handsomely.  I relished this.  Thank you!
Pretty good puzzle.  Learned something new:  Have lived on COASTAL New England all my life, but have never heard of CRAB LOUIE.

The expression OSCARBAIT was new to me, which might explain why I didn't see the full theme (fish on hooks) until after I had finished.

Clever theme, but not a Thursday puzzle. Wednesday for sure. 

"What "R" might stand for on an envelope" for RHODE stumped me for too long, as did "Toys for tots, perhaps" for TYPO. But too many of the clues were too direct, so I was done in less than 20 minutes. 

Nice puzzle---but not for a Thursday.

Good morning! I got the fish in the shaded boxes, but didn't even stop to consider the circled letters. 
I really should pay more attention to construction - this was simply elegant. 
Thanks for a lovely Thursday.
I make this observation with bated breath, but 50A was a bit of a revealer as well, since most hits on pop charts feature a good hook.
Good theme but had to resort to the comments to crack the double natick in the SE and keep my streak going
Am I the first to comment on the lovely column? I thought Sean's description of the multiple entryways to solving was quite Poe-etic. And true to my experience.

Typical tough Thursday for me, and must confess that I really wasn't catching on to the theme at all until I after I had it all filled in. No big deal - that's just me. And of course a puzzle find today. A Wednesday from March 17, 1999 by Myles Callum. Three grid-spanning theme entries in that one. One clue and answer example: "Basketball's Archibald injured the Dalai Lama, palindromically." NATEBITATIBETAN And the other two theme answers: BOMBARDADRABMOB TOOBADIHIDABOOT Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=3/17/1999&g=36&d=A Oh, and NATEBITATIBETAN was a clue in one other puzzle - a Wednesday from February 14, 1996 by C. Lundberg. The two other theme answers in that one: ABLEROFFFORELBA SLUGOMAHAMOGULS I'm done. ....
Nothing fishy about this one at all!  I thoroughly enjoyed today's solve.  Thank you Mr. Enfinger and Mr. Kugelman.

As someone from Ogden, that is the last waterfall in Utah that even occurred to me because it’s man made. It’s just spillage from a pipeline. It’s not real. Does anyone outside the state even know about that waterfall?

So are the fish names related to the movies at all?
Did Tim Salmon (outfielder for the California Angels) make a cameo in Rain Man, etc.?

Definitely benefitted from sleeping on this one.  Woke up and what seemed unsolvable last night suddenly was right there. (Still can't believe how many letters I had before SHREK dawned.) Even though it wasn't a "Tricky" Thursday per se, there were plenty of tricky clues.  I had fun solving.  Thanks, guys.

PS Never heard the phrase DO A good turn. I had One for a very long time.
Wow! I was just about to give up on this one, when I realized that 55D could be VOWS instead of “Idos.” From there, everything fell into place ten minutes under my average!

This was a nice, tricky puzzle.  It had some good misdirects, a lot of unknowns and uncertains for me, which I like.  I want some crunch in my puzzle.
Yet,...

I saw the revealer, I got the theme, and I can see that it's clever, but...
Somehow I'm still unimpressed.
I want more on a Thursday.

I blame myself.
Thursday joy! (As opposed to Thursday grr haha)

It's interesting to note that the1997 legal drama film The RAINMAKER did not receive any Academy Award nominations. 4D is clued as [Hotshot lawyer], an interesting term for me, meaning a lawyer who brings lots of cases and $$$ to a firm. The film was written and directed by Francis Ford Coppola based on a novel by John Grisham. It featured an amazing ensemble cast including Matt Damon, Danny DeVito, Jon Voight, Claire Danes, Mary Kay Place, Mickey Rourke, Danny Glover, Roy Scheider, Virginia Madsen, Teresa Wright, Dean Stockwell. Even singer Randy Travis appeared in the film. Travis was known for a great song, IMHO, called "King of the Road." https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=60dhFctdMKM "Trailer for sale or rent Room to let 50 cents I'm a man of means By no means King of the Road" I saw the film years ago and although the legal stuff was very interesting, I found the film very scary. Another film item in the puzzle: 1A [Grammy-winning singer who's had both a "Farewell Tour" and a "Here We Go Again Tour"]--CHER. Cher starred in a favorite film of mine: "Burlesque." Also with an amazing ensemble cast, it received a nod at the Golden Globes. Music and dance, also starring Christina Aguilera, Stanley Tucci, Kristen Bell, and more.

The only things making this puzzle Thursday-difficult were c;iues about GenZ and Mario stuff.  I don't even know if my own children are Z's (or X, Y, or what-all.)  I have never had anything to do with those Mario brothers, though once I did play Pac-Man...and was reduced to holleriing, "Run!!"  

Here's hoping we don't have another debate about the utterings of baby goats vs. baby sheep.  Bah!

The clue at 41A gets my vote for The Winner/Best Stumper of the Puzzle...possibly of The Year!  RHONE wanted that spot SO badly, but I knew it could never be correct....and even when I finally (and I do mean FINALly) entered the D, it took me a second to understand.  Good one, fellas.

Happy to be home, but...wow, Seattle was lovely!

At first I ignored both the tiny little circles and the gray squares.   But when I was really, really struggling in the SE, I thought that maybe those gray squares down there could help me.  So I looked at those that I'd already filled in and I saw...FISH!!!  So now I needed a 4-letter fish beginning with C.  Aha -- CHAR!!!  And thus my Big Trouble in the SE was overcome.  And I saw what the problem was:

The answer to "Shortchange" isn't DECEIVE.  It's DEPRIVE.

And thus I solved the puzzle without noticing the movies in the tiny little circles.  Didn't need them and therefore didn't care -- though I suppose it's a praiseworthy, intricate bit of construction.

There's a nice Nancy-clue at 45D.  Those of you who know my penchant for heaving puzzles I don't like against the wall will probably have picked up on that.  But, happily, this puzzle didn't make a sound.

I love the clue for PATE (11D).  Knowing BEN HOGAN was a big help to me.  Today I learned that AETNA is named after the volcano (odd choice, AETNA).  And clues for TYPO like the one at 22D fool me every single time!

I've never heard the term OSCAR BAIT, btw.  Doesn't every film try to appeal to the Oscar voters?  How do you bait your movie hook in some special way?  Beats me.

A puzzle I quite enjoyed -- even while ignoring half the theme.

Wow, two Wednesday puzzles in a row this week.

Y for T is my most common TYPO when solving on my laptop - those keys are next to each other.

I have to use a black background with white letters. The circles don't show up in the greyed squares. It was only after I finished and saw there were circled letters that I realized something was odd and read the gameplay comments that I was able to figure out that the circled letters made a hook to catch the fish in the shaded boxes.  Cute idea.
Once again I never noticed the revealer, ignored the gray circles and managed to muddle through. Too old to change?
My Bubbie used to make chopped liver with sautéed onions and schmaltz. No, PATE, you are definitely not it.

This was nice and chewy but still pretty straightforward for Thurs. Felt like a great Weds puzzle. I was reading the hooks left to right and thought NOT TAP was some oscar winner I had never heard of!

the construction is impressive but the absurd amounts of trivia just kills the enjoyment of these puzzles. there's nothing to solve ... you either know the trivia or you just shrug and look it up. the nytxw and jeopardy! venn diagram continues to increase in overlap.
I strongly disagree with Sean's take on the cluing for 41A. How anyone was expected to deduce that it referred to "Rhode" Island is beyond me.

This was fun. RHODE was the hardest for me to get, but well I am not from the US so maybe that's why. I was toying with reorg and roore for so long before I realised I have written DEPRIVED as REPRIVED idk why. Guess I made an actual TYPO today 😂


I had solved the Wednesday just before this and I really disliked that puzzle due to its top right section. But today's puzzle was fun, putting pieces down here and there in a calculated way that solves the whole thing in the end is what crosswords are all about.

The trivia was a bit heavy but I guess that is just Thursday.
Fun! SE took a second but oscar bait is worth it as a theme
"Toys for tots, perhaps" is obviously TOPS, right? Couldn't be POTS... Wait a minute – TYPO was such a great AHA moment!
I find it dubious that any of the routes that ISLANDAIR flew would actually take them over Mauna Kea…

19 day streak but the big news is: 2000
Puzzles Solved! Thought you fellow cruciverbalists could appreciate that! Nice puzzle
Atrocious clues

This was in my wheelhouse, partly because I was familiar with the golfer, the government org, and the type face. Figured out RHODE when I had RHO_E crossing _EPRIVE, a fun little aha moment.

Today I had the time to pause before filling the last square to look back and figure out the theme. Saw there were fish and hooks, and the hooks were movies, but had to find the revealer again to figure out their relationship. That was a nicely quizzical moment at the end of my solve.

Gotta get back to work. May have to wait until the weekend to read what the rest of you have to say. Cheers!
That was fun. I really liked it. Difficult answers could be puzzled out with the help of crosses.  There was more than one theme element. Not too easy. Nice puzzle.

Late answer history search. Wondered about... DOTCOM as part of an answer, and.. 12 answers that start with that have been in Shortz era puzzles. Some examples: 

DOTCOMBOOM
DOTCOMBUST
DOTCOMCRASH
DOTCOMICS
DOTCOMMA
DOTCOMPARABLE
DOTCOMPOSER
DOTCOMPOST
DOTCOMPRESS
DOTCOMPROMISE

Whew. 

....
The Mechanical Turk reminds me a lot of "AI" which often ends up not being the work of an LLM but the labor of some Kenyan or Indian after all.

I may be expecting too much but I would've liked to have seen more of a connection between the particular hook (the movie) and either the fish or the cross containing the fish.  It seemed a bit random and not really bait worthy.  I was snagged but not landed.
Re: EMBALMER. Is it the case that embalming is common in the US? Just curious. In my entire life here I've never come across it being employed, or even mentioned.

Loved all of the movie themed clues in this one.

The gimmick didn’t do much for me, but they rarely do, so hey.

Perhaps a tad on the easy side for this late into the week, though the SE corner was brutal. 

Putting the name of an old golf player as the vanguard to a trivia-heavy section made it pretty impenetrable. 

Why it wasn’t included in the Wordplay column as a tricky clue, even when it was alluded to, is beyond me. 

Not bad, not great. Happy Thursday!
I thought the construction was clever and unexpected.  Thanks!

A bunch of restaurants in Manhattan serve crab louis, not to mention those in the "outer"( speaking of provincial) boroughs. But, hey, New Yorkers, the very center of the universe is way up in the left hand corner of our great nation!
A bit of a natick for me at the cross of 53 down and 61 across. If you don’t know the spellings, you don’t hear the music. Had to consult the answer key. But… so it goes in crossword puzzling.
This was perfectly delightful. Just right. Thank you!
I enjoyed the puzzle but I looked up crab Louie and am upset now.  Thats just a Cobb salad with crab.  Is the bar for salad names so low?

I love both salmon and trout but I've never had char. I just looked it up and learned that it's in the same family and the three are very closely related. Thought that was kind of neat. I'm going to have to find us some char to try.  

Enjoyed the clues for pate, obey, and drama. Some other fun ones. A fine puzzle!

I have learned many things from doing the crosswords these last couple years, but I don't think I shall ever, no matter how many I do, remember the names or initialisms of game consoles. It's never caused any real problems but I don't know why it doesn't stick like so many other things do. Probably because I don't have a way to reference it, like when I learned agora, agoraphobia, fear of the marketplace... Voila!  

Oh well, pobody's nerfect!

Today's poem made from words found in today’s puzzle

a/ we go the way of all things
the past leaves the bones
in the tombs left
the name of the egyptian king carved in to

never
a billy goat’s cry
d/ and we are here perhaps
and what am i perhaps a parade of words
brief new i you awesome good put away
a/ come close
stay

I agree with with Sean that this puzzle had a trivia feeling to it! I really enjoyed that though, it was very fun. I love a puzzle that leaves you with a few new fun facts.

Got held up in the southeast corner like a lot of folks, but with a little patience things filled in from the crosses. I did have one lookup; I had no clue what the king of salads was.

The puzzle was fun enough.  However, it feels like another "everything but the kitchen sink" puzzle after the fact.

I'm sure there was a lot Skill that went into constructing the puzzle, but there have been a lot of recent puzzles where the constructorsare trying to do too much.

I solved this without lookups slightly faster than my usual Thursday solve. However, I think this puzzle is best featured in the trash can.

The theme is atrocious. After solving, I came to the wordplay column hoping for clarity and the best I can get is the circled letters (award winners) formed a hook? 

Next comes the sloppy clueing. 41A. What “R” might stand for on an envelope? RHODE? When is it EVER listed as just RHODE?

Thankfully the crosses were easy enough. This wasn’t challenging, it was annoying.

Rating:
2/10

I thought the theme was great, even though I didn't pick up on the movie names being hooks for the fish. I noticed the two thematic categories separately.

I also enjoyed the puns and misdirects. Unfortunately for me, trivia clues crossing trivia clues is my weakness, so I had to do some half-cheating Googling (e.g., "golfer Ben H").

Overall, I enjoyed it, and I can't relate to all the huffing and puffing in the comments.

My usual late puzzle find. A Sunday from August 11, 1996 by M. Schneider with the title "The Critics Agree." A couple of theme clue and answer examples: "LETHARGIC" LAZYSUSANSARANDON "SICKENING" TYPHOIDMARYPICKFORD And some other theme answers: MUSTANGSALLYFIELD PRETTYPENNYMARSHALL CALAMITYJANEFONDA LOVELYRITAHAYWORTH Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=8/11/1996&g=50&d=A ....
"The King of Salads" sounds like the most backhanded compliment ever.

I just can’t figure out what’s wrong with my MIDI puzzle today. Did New Yorker’s May 4th by Will Nediger instead of today’s NYT XWP as of yet.
There was quite an odd answer in there for “Seeing double?” and the answers aren’t out yet but I think I got it. I hope to do today’s if all goes well!
I enjoyed this one and solved it in my usual Thursday time. But, even having read the Gameplay, I still don't get why "toys for tots" solves to "typo." I know I'm missing the wordplay there....
Didn’t love the theme at all, but the clueing was great. Maybe I’d have liked it better if the films related to fish/fishing? I felt like I was missing something.

It is late at night, so I didn’t read the column or scour the comments, so forgive me if this was explained. Who spells it CRAB LOUIE (and not LOUIS, which I entered immediately on my first pass). Is that a regional thing? Also, I was slightly irked by all the theme answers reading counter-clockwise except PATTON (for which I kept wondering which movie I was unfamiliar with called NOTTAP). 

Was the “theme” about angling supposed to refer to the answers forming a fishing hook shape? And were the three fish supposed to be Oscar bait? Do Oscars (the fish) prey upon those three fish? Ok, I guess I can buy that, in retrospect. Did not help with the solve at all. 

At the end, I said, “OK, I guess.” Normally, I enjoy saying, “Wow, clever!” No need to reply, just venting because it wasn’t the typically fun, pun-filled Thursday puzzle.
Is there any connection between the fish and the movie? I.e., what does Salmon have to do with Rain Man?

What a fun puzzle!  I *loved* the cluing.  Several answers completely eluded me last night, so I left the puzzle and went to bed.  My mind must have worked on it in my sleep because this morning every one was obvious to me.

My favorite “ah ha!” moment was the “R on an envelope.”  I grew up in a rural area, so I knew it had to be either RURAL or ROUTE.  (Rural postal customers in the US are grouped into rural routes, so an address might be “RR 3.”). I figured I’d decide between RURAL and ROUTE with the crossings.  Of course, the crossings didn’t cooperate, so I went to bed frustrated.  This morning it was obvious.  What fun!
@Evan same, and not being a golf historian didn’t help me, either.  I was struggling with “BENHAGAN” way too long.

@Evan

Right? All that trivia in one place, Jesus...
Ok I looked at the theme. I get it but that’s quite the stretch for a hook (pun intended).

@Selective Walrus
For the first time, I had problems with a visual trick. On my smartphone's screen, the circles on grayed-out letters were invisible. Even with enlargement, they were still undetectable.  So my supposed hooks were just a buncha letters: RAIN-N, PA-T-N, and BEN-U. Annoyong. 🤨

@SBK in TO

It's either time for a new phone or an eye exam.  I had no trouble seeing the circles (although I originally solved on my desktop).

Hmmm, let's see...
1. You angered the emu over your use of such an elementary descriptor? 
2. Payback over your recent NYMag quote casting aspersions on their (alleged) puzzle error?

I had a month-long ordeal with the NYTCrossword chatbot before I was finally assigned a human (Eric E.). Nothing was being posted with no explanation offered. Lots of back-and-forth (mostly forth) until resolution was reached. No reason was given. I thanked Eric E. and never received a reply.
The closest I was able do drag out of Eric E. was their filters get full and occasionally have to be purged. Maybe I was reported so often I was flagged(?)
Truthfully, the break from commenting was a blessing in disguise.😛
Hope you continue educating the aspiring cruciverbalist in all of us.

Jerry,
The filter blocked my original post that contained that one word, and my reply in this thread that also used it. When a mod saw that I wasn't attacking a person, both posts were released. It wasn't personal; it was just business.

@The X-Phile
Kind of like the random TSA screenings we have all, at one time or another, been victims of.

Steve L,

Type A for Type B, perhaps?

@Steve L

So what did you think of the payoff here?

I more or less ignored the circled letters until I was finished, and then I saw the film titles. 

Sean McGowan describes how BEN-HUR helped him get BEN HOGAN. I had no idea who the golfer was (though I recognized the name) and BEN was not the part for which I most could have used some help.

@Eric Hougland

Well, I agreed with SP’s assessment that this was a “constructor’s puzzle,” so that should give you some idea.

@Steve L

Thanks.

I saw SP’s comment (which I too agree with) after I asked your thoughts (which was really a rhetorical question in the first place). 

I commented elsewhere on the thematic similarities between this puzzle and a WaPo puzzle from a few months back. Having seen the earlier iteration of this theme, I don’t think I can honestly judge this one entirely on its own merits.

@Eric Hougland

I used to do a variety of other crosswords every day, but with the advent of Connections, Wordle, Spelling Bee (which takes up more time than any three crosswords) and some other non-NYT puzzles I do, I just do NYT and the NYer these days.

Orcs are coroners, since they have knowledge in Mordor cases. Tom Bombadil was a weight loss surgeon since he specialized in Barrow-atrics. The only OB Gyn I know of in LOTR was Earendil the Mariner since he studied sea sections and berth control.

@SP
Pro-CHAR piscivore here. The further north you go, the likelier  you are to see it on the menu. It is really delicious and, as northern waters require, it is luscious and fatty and therefore gorgeous when smoked. If heaven doesn't have goldeye on the menu (let's say they've run out for that day),  I'll settle for CHAR.

@SP

In Polish "kitel" (which we pronounce the same as you do "kittle") is the protective coat worn by doctors or lab workers.

@Andrzej

I guess that’s (appropriately enough) another kitel of fish

@SP

🙄

@SBK in TO

Char is delicious. I’ve normally seen it on menus, and in crosswords, as Arctic Char.
@Andrzej ha! Exactly the same in Danish. Love that, as there aren't that many words Danes and Poles have in common.

@Søren Thustrup

It's a loan word from German, and I'm sure our languages share more of those.

@SP

“But, realistically, you have to admit that otherwise this entirely solved like a themeless (and not a hard one at that) so I have to suggest that it is more suited for a Wednesday.”

Agreed. The themers would have needed to cross some exceedingly hard/obscure entries to be of any use in the solve…in which case the relative obscurity of CHAR and the reversed orientation of the PATTON “bait” (i.e. hook) might have complicated things a bit.

But regardless, it’s a fun-ish idea, and well executed.

@Andrzej
Aha! In that case, it is probably kin to  'coat' in English. Reverse engineering linguistically, the 'el' in kitel is a diminutive. So it's a little 'kit'. But the change in stress with that suffix also results in a vowel shift. What's now a short I would have previously been a long O. So K-long O-T becomes K-short I-T-ə-L.

@Matt

How are they similar? Kugelman is all about cringey dad jokes (well, not today, but in 9 out of 10 of his pained creations), pseudo-witty clues and obscure trivia. Ezersky never does groaners (that I remember), and his clues are actually often quite intelligently misleading. I suppose the use of niche proper names is what they are both guilty of, but beyond that? Their grids are nothing alike.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Andrzej

They are both quotidian, grounded and meat-and-potatoes setters.

Their differences are like the differences between accountants and patent attorneys, say.

The music of the spheres does not vibrate through their constructions.

Weintraub and Goldstein are the Platonists of grids and are all about flirting with and approaching the forms of clue and fill.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Andrzej

And let me get this straight:  you're saying three movie names shaped like fish hooks (which are not OSCARBAIT, incidentally, but rather naked, bait-free barbs) which have caught three different types of fish is not a groany dad joke?

"The music of the spheres does not vibrate through their constructions."

Fair enough, that's a good point. However, it applies to almost all constructors, so while it is true for both Kugelman and Ezersky, it is not their differentia specifica in the pool of setters.

I just don't see how today's theme could be considered groany. Granted, it's questionable (I mean, usually the bait is not bigger than the fish, and it's no longer on the hook once the fish has swallowed it), but groany? cringey? I don't see it, and my groaner radar is one of the most sensitive ones around.

Also, while I get why many solvers enjoy Weintraub puzzles, personally I usually don't (for my typical reason - overreliance on niche trivia and words not in my vocabulary; this is down to who I am though and no fault of Ms Weintraub; I *see* she has an elegant style, it's just that she defeats me with it almost every time, and as a rather simple soul I just don't enjoy losing🤣)

commentBody:
@Andrzej

A certain good-natured and jaunty comfort with being bested by special women is one of the keys to being an evolved man.

@Matt

That is a very good point.
@Abby I've never seen it spelled louis. YMMV

@Steven M.
Interesting. I’ve almost always seen it spelled Louis. Perhaps a function of my not frequenting restaurants in recent years (noise levels drove me out. Prices and too many trendy places helped).
@Chris T-O-Y-S is not how to spell “tots.” If you accidentally spell tots that way, you’ve made a TYPO.

@Chris One might accidentally type a “y” instead of an “o” when  trying to write TOTS, thus ending up with TOYS instead. 
Toys for tots.

@Steve Kass
TYPO was my bête noire. When I couldn't get to the win and was flyspecking, I fell back into a bad habit. I just hunted for obvious misspellings without looking at the clues. 
I knew I hadn't understood some clues but I'd gotten TYP from crosses, so confidently I'd filled in E and moved on. It was only many minutes later that I realized that DeTBOMB made no sense. So I gutted that block and reconstructed. Success, belatedly.
And in the Dept. of Irony, I had just completed the archived puzzle for Feb 15 2022, for which the trick turns on the idea of the DOTCOM industry.

@Chris
The clue uses two meanings of 'for'.
1. To be given to, as a gift. That's the natural meaning in the clue as phrased.
2. In place of, as a substitute. That's the trick reading. If you've typed t-o-y-s in place of t-o-t-s, that's a TYPO.
As anyone who's learned a second language can testify, it's the infuriating little words -- conjunctions, prepositions -- that are crucial to meaning and also easily confused.

@SBK in TO
Well, that's embarrassing. My time travel was unconnected to the DOTBOMB/DOTCOM battle. I mistook yesterday's puzzle for my archived puzzle, which was free of DOTCOMs.

@Cat Lady Margaret

Thank you so much for sharing this. What a beautiful song.
@M - I treat the puzzles as scavenger hunts.  I look up the trivia I don't know and move on.

@M "I think I'm going to get into cryptic I'm sick of having to google arbitrary bs."  I recently tried to get into cryptic crosswords, and this statement is the funniest thing I've read in a while!  (If intentional, I applaud you; if not, here's why I laughed...)

The word "sailor" in a clue can imply the letters "AB" in the answer (for Able Boatman, I think?) and the word "book" can imply the letters "NT" (for New Testament).  That's where I gave up, but I wish you luck.

@M

What you call "trivia" I call the base of general knowledge in the current US.  Same thing, different attitude.

What I don't know, I learn.  And what I'm not interested in, I learn to get better at crosswords, and be able to act like I have some idea of what people are talking about at parties.

Someone complained about BEN HOGAN, a golfer whose last tournament win was sometime in the 50s.   But he was a major star (think along the lines of Babe Ruth in baseball) and is still known as the eponym for a line of golf products.  I have never watched golf, nor was I alive when Hogan was a star, but somehow I knew his name. 

How?  I have no idea.

Learning trivia is like batting practice in baseball.

@Kerry JK

Based on participation here, it seems a lot of Brits (not to mention Aussies and other international solvers) like American-style crosswords.

@Steve L
I can tell you how you knew Hogan's name. I suspect that you read a lot. I suspect that most of us do. And when you read, things collect in your brain like dust bunnies under a couch.
My family subscribed to the New Yorker magazine for many years. I have never watched a game of golf but I read the golf column -- because it was beautifully written by Herbert Warren Wind. Likewise their horse racing column by Audax Minor. And their fashion columns.  And their "Talk of the Town." 
But in absorbing the literature, I also absorbed facts and ideas aplenty about subjects in which I was completely uninterested, and there they are today, still.

@Steve L
What things are worthy of our attention could be a long philosophical debate. Thank you for responding.

@Steve L Here is an excellent example of a Rube Goldberg machine. https://d.ibtimes.co.uk/en/full/1665212/rube-goldberg-machine.jpg There are three requirements: 1. The task must trivial and easily accomplished by ordinary means. 2. The means of accomplishment using the machine must be elaborated and fantasticated beyond belief. 3. At least one step in the process must use a living thing as a mechanical element -- e.g., cat chasing mouse, man swatting fly, etc. Many have tried to reproduce the delightful machinations but the inimitable Goldbergian style is beyond compare. Accept No Substitutes.

@sotto voce

Hall & OATES were at the peak of their fame during my high school days. They’ve never been favorites of mine (sorry; can we still be friends?).

I managed to not listen to them for decades until one ski trip to Utah about 10 years ago. Every single time we were in the cafeteria at Brighton (our favorite resort), we heard at least one or two Hall & Oates songs. (You don’t need oldies radio to hear “classic rock.” You just need to hang out in ski resorts.)

Have some salmon for me. I love it. We don’t get it much in the Four Corners area. (Though I’m told that the place where we buy the  fantastic beef we eat too often also has great seafood flown in twice a week. Not PNW fresh, but on the plus side, it’s almost always sunny here.)

@Eric Hougland

So great to see you back! You've been missed!

We can absolutely still be friends. I was also never much of a fan of Hall & Oates. The puzzle sent me on a listening expedition and I was reminded why, lol.

The only good thing about listening to them again was all the great memories of the 80's that it brought back, none of them related to Hall &Oates.

Okay, so you win on the sunshine front, but you'll just have to come visit the PNW for the best fresh salmon ever!

@Vaer

This is way funkier and better than the original. Thanks for posting it!

ISLAND AIR also did not serve New York, and OGDEN isn't in New York. The New York Times Crossword is not that provincial.

I first thought of DELMAR from yesterday's puzzle when I read your comment. Then you should know that you can get the most delicious whole Dungeness crabs in the supermarket out West. Yum. All the movies in the puzzle were most likely made in California. (Hollywood is not in New York.) RIELS and Angkor Wat are from some place else. As are the EMIRs from Qatari. RHODE Island isn't in New York. ABBA, certainly, is from far away. Kenrick Lamar of DAMN, BENHOGAN, CHER, RUBE Goldberg, and OATES of Hall and Oates may not be New Yorkers. Your rules are pretty strict. Speaking of Texan Ben Hogan, by the way--here is another film item from the puzzle: there is a movie called "Follow the Sun" about his life: https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0043550/

@Kerry JK

And it’s the unchecked squares that l dislike most about cryptic crosswords (though the tortured syntax in some of the clues puts me off, too).

@Eric Hougland
"UNCH" is very commonly seen in UK cross-talk.

@Eric Hougland
Sorry to sound dim, but what is an unchecked square, and what is meant by getting rid of them?

@sotto voce
Thank you very much! Yes, a typical cryptic does have unchecked squares; there are fewer clues, and the shape of the grid is completely irrelevant regarding eg symmetry. Each clue is like a world on its own, no themes.

@Jane Wheelaghan
There can be themes in cryptics but usually accompanied in the intro with an explanation, so you're not completely ambushed. And they can be real rippers! "We've removed one word from every DOWN clue and placed it in an ACROSS clue." Or " All ACROSS answers are missing an article." And you discover that A, AN, or THE have been excised from the solves, so that FEATHER goes into the grid as FEAR. Etc.

Eric Hougland,

“…cast no aspersions…”

Speaking of stealing someone’s ideas, I was just about to post something something about “the lure of an ensemble cast”. Now it’ll never fly.

I've been to Ogden, Utah, but never knew about the waterfall. Today's Google Doodle celebrates the 100th Anniversary of Route 66. But on that trip, we didn't take Route 66 but a northern route. My co-driver had family in Ogden. We went through Reno; Ogden; Jackson Hole; Grand Tetons; Yellowstone; Cody, Wyoming (home to a great museum of Western art); on to Rapid City, South Dakota, where we were almost in a flood; Ann Arbor, Michigan (where my co-driver had attended university); Chicago; Pennsylvania where there was so much rain that the vehicles formed a convoy driving slowly and in order behind the lead vehicle; and onto, by some miracle, New York.

Huh? 
No! Of course not! The vast majority of films try to appeal only to numerous ticket-buyers.
'Oscar bait' refers (I think) to self- consciously Serious Drama, often replete with period costumes and/or Serious Actors portraying the Differently Abled. It's a specific genre.

@Charles Peterson -- Interesting criteria -- but none of the Best Picture winners that immediately spring to mind, at least those from the last century, meet them.  Lawrence of Arabia; The Sting; Kramer Vs Kramer; The Sound of Music; The Apartment; The Godfather; West Side Story, to name a few.  Maybe you're referring to the kinds of ROLES, portraying "differently abled" characters, that garner awards for Best Actor or Actress?  Like, say, Dustin Hoffman in Rain Man?

Or maybe things have changed completely in the streaming, Netflix era and very different kinds of films are winning Best Picture?  And I don't know about it because these aren't the kinds of movies I gravitate to and/or I haven't been paying attention?  I'm certainly not familiar with any of this as a "specific genre".  But then I haven't watched the Oscars in years -- the program bores me to death.

@Barry Ancona As a non-American I’ve always felt there are too many American geographic clues vs.ones for the rest of the world. I think more international references would be more challenging for Americans while more accessible to the rest of us.

@Chaz L. To clarify further, "Toys for Tots" is a charitable endeavor begun in 1947 by the USMC in which Marine reservists and local organizations (i.e. fire or police departments, schools, scout troops, sports teams, etc.) collect donations of toys to be distributed among children (tots) in disadvantaged communities, after natural disasters such as fires or floods, or for other families in need. So the clue was a misdirect in a way, leading a solver to search fruitlessly for a synonym or other term of equivalence. Toys for Tots - Wikipedia https://share.google/HcresxBqcjlNE7lmh

@Jane Wheelaghan
If the burial occurs within 3-4 days, embalming is normally not done. It adds significant cost to the funeral, even if embalming  becomes necessary.

@Jane Wheelaghan I believe the technicians who do embalming also know how to do other (don't think too long about this one) body-prep tasks. They are not necessarily called embalmers. I attended a community college summer class attended by several funeral science students.

Embalming is of great use to those in physiology or anatomy classes. I remember embalmed pigs in one lab. They are easier to deal with than formaldehyde-stored cadavers.

@Jane Wheelaghan

I, in my dark thoughts, always thought embalming was a way of insuring that there isn't an accidental premature burial.

Bob,
It's been a while, but I enjoyed said crab without the louis (or louie) on the Dungeness Spit. That was a year after I took the ferry to Orcas.

Barry, you probably were at Three Crabs, now defunct (actually now reclaimed as a center for salmon habitat restoration). From me youth o'er the span of years to emerging old age, I ate at the Three Crabs dozens of times, more often than not the louis/louie. What's not to like about  ice-cold picked crab with olives, eggs, tomatoes and greens with any kind of red dressing? Throw in a decent baguette and  a good white Pinot and you've got something.

From my front window, I can see the dock from which you took your Orcas ferry.  If you're going to be in the neighborhood again, look me up. Sounds like you've been here more than once.

I was at Three Crabs in 1972; I know it's gone. I was in and out of the PNW from 1971 to 1986 ("turn out the lights") for a variety of reasons. I know quite a bit has changed there since then. And everywhere else. Regards.

The R clue is misdirection.  It simply asks what it could stand for.  It never says it is used alone.  You inferred that, and that's on you.  It's not fair to blame the puzzle because you were caught by something puzzling.
